segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dynamic animated sequence for the 'CHANNELS' brand, featuring bold red and blue 3D typography and a stylized map of Africa within a circular emblem, accompanied by an upbeat, dramatic orchestral score. The logo transitions through various visual effects, including a glowing neon 'REPORTING TONIGHT' sign and a nighttime exterior shot of a building with the CHANNELS logo illuminated in red. A male voiceover announces, "From Lagos, the nation's commercial capital. This is the News at 10. Live from Channels Television. Reporting tonight, Ayodele Balogun." The sequence culminates in a modern news studio where a male news anchor, dressed in a light gray suit with a striped tie and brown shoes, stands beside a large screen displaying the 'NEWS at 10' logo. The studio features a sleek curved desk, vibrant blue and white lighting, and a city skyline backdrop. Holding a tablet, the anchor gestures as he greets viewers with "Hello and welcome." He then begins to report on a headline, stating, "Tonight, more buildings to go down in Lagos Island, declares Governor Babajide Sanwo-Olu, as he vows to go after those flouting safety regulations..." The screen behind him transitions to a split view, showing a reporter on-site with a red microphone and footage of a fire-ravaged market, labeled 'LAGOS MARKET FIRE AFTERMATH'. A lower third graphic displays the headline: 'UMAHI: LAGOS-CALABAR COASTAL ROAD WILL COST N15TR', with the time '22:00' visible, while the anchor continues to speak.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of Reporter Dari Du, a man wearing a cap, glasses, and a face mask, holding a red microphone and speaking to a crowd in Lagos Island, as an initial male voice narrates about the state's fire agencies addressing safety regulations in the commercial hub, following an inferno that affected about 14 buildings. Lower Third Graphics display 'MORE BUILDINGS TO GO DOWN LAGOS ISLAND' and '22:02'. The scene transitions to aerial footage of the Fire-Ravaged Market, revealing widespread destruction with thick smoke rising from charred debris and collapsed structures, while the Lower Third Graphics update to 'DOSUMU FIRE DISASTER' and 'Sanwo-Olu Visits Scene In Lagos Island'. Amidst the chaotic and smoky environment, Reporter Dari Du's voice, with a distinct accent, takes over, describing the scene over background sounds of water spraying and crackling, noting that 'Over 48 hours after, it is still a live site here at Dosumu Market,' and that 'The smoke, although not as heavy as that of the day one, still filters in the air.' Ground-level shots show Firefighters in yellow helmets and reflective gear actively spraying water onto smoldering ruins near a red fire truck, as Dari Du mentions 'firemen continuing with the damping down.' A group of Officials and Emergency Personnel, including men in yellow safety vests and hard hats, are then seen walking through the site, inspecting the damage, with Dari Du reporting that 'Governor Babajide Sanwo-Olu is making his first visit to this site since this particular incident,' a 'familiar scene especially at this market.' The CHANNELS Logo remains in the top left corner, and news tickers like 'ECB KEEPS INTEREST RATE UNCHANGED AT RECORD 4%' and 'NFL GREAT OJ SIMPSON DIES OF CANCER, AGED 76' scroll across the Lower Third Graphics, contributing to the somber atmosphere reflecting the disaster's aftermath. The reporter concludes by mentioning the governor announced some traders lost valuables in the inferno before the audio cuts.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a chaotic, crowded scene at the Dosumu Fire Disaster site in Lagos Island, where firefighters in yellow helmets and reflective vests are actively managing the aftermath near a red 'LAGOS STATE FIRE SERVICE' truck. The dense crowd, many wearing face masks, creates a tense and urgent atmosphere. A male voice, identified as the Reporter on-site, begins speaking, stating, "What I have seen here is what could have been prevented... initial reports... was just gross carelessness." The camera cuts to a close-up of the Reporter on-site, a man in a cap, glasses, and face mask, holding a red microphone, flanked by a woman with a yellow microphone and others. On-screen graphics display 'DOSUMU FIRE DISASTER' and 'Sanwo-Olu Visits Scene In Lagos Island'. The Reporter continues, explaining his intent to "speak to the other stakeholders and hear how what amount of support, what level of comfort... will be rendered to some of the people that have been affected," and how they will "settle them very quickly and see how we can get them back on their feet." An aerial view briefly shows the fire-ravaged market with extensive destruction, charred debris, and smoke, visually reinforcing the disaster's scale. The video returns to the Reporter, whose background remains crowded with officials and emergency personnel. The lower third updates to 'Sanwo-Olu: More Buildings Will Go Down In Lagos Island', alongside foreign news tickers like 'RUSSIAN STRIKES HIT ODESA ON LIBERATION DAY'. The Reporter concludes, emphasizing, "But this is an incident too often, very, very condemnable and very unacceptable." The CHANNELS Logo is consistently in the top left, and the NEWS at 10 Logo appears intermittently in the lower third, maintaining a serious and urgent tone throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with aerial footage of a devastated urban area in Lagos Island, showing widespread destruction from a fire, with smoke rising from charred buildings and debris, while emergency vehicles and crowds are visible on the streets. A male voice, identified as the Reporter on-site, begins by stating that "The governor is worried that residents and traders here have continually flouted safety regulations, mandating state agencies to attend to these issues without delay." The scene transitions to a ground-level report where the Reporter on-site, wearing a cap, glasses, and a face mask, holds a red microphone and speaks to a group including the Man in Beige Shirt and Sunglasses, the Man in Green Cap, and the Woman with Yellow Microphone. The Reporter continues, announcing that "The market all around will be closed today, tomorrow, until when we are able to do a full assessment and to do a proper cleanup of this entire area." The crowd, some wearing face masks and casual attire, listens intently as the Reporter delivers updates, with damaged structures and makeshift shelters visible in the background. He adds, "I want to say without any houter of doubt that some other buildings are still going to go down. This is totally unacceptable. We will not allow a few people, a few people who will not comply with our rules, with our laws to put the lives of others in danger." Lower third graphics display sports headlines like 'PL TO USE SEMI-AUTOMATED OFFSIDE TECH. NEXT SEASON' and 'BURNLEY MANAGER KOMPANY BANNED FOR TWO GAMES', while the CHANNELS Logo remains in the top left corner. The Reporter further warns that "Fiscal planning, planning authorities, LAPCA are going to be having a difficult time with me. And when they have a difficult time with me, you will know they will come on the street," before the audio cuts off with "At least 40..." as the segment concludes with another aerial view of the smoldering ruins, emphasizing the scale of destruction.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with aerial footage of a devastated urban area, showing widespread destruction, collapsed buildings, debris, and rising smoke, with emergency vehicles like a green excavator and red fire trucks visible. A male voice, identified as Darry Adigun of Channels Television News, reports on structures destroyed in an inferno on Lagos Island, mentioning it as one of the highest numbers of buildings affected in one incident and recalling a similar fire at the nearby Balogun Market in 2020. The lower third initially displays a sports headline about Dutch skiers. The scene then transitions to a modern news studio, where the News Anchor, dressed in a light gray suit and striped tie, stands before a city skyline backdrop. segments.integrated_caption: The scene captures a live outdoor political event in Rivers State, Nigeria, broadcast by Channels TV, under natural daylight. His clear, authoritative voice declares the need 'to defend the interest of all Rivers people in accordance with the Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria.' He praises an individual for 'standing on firm ground and with the strength and courage of a lion,' adding, 'We are, we are, we are proud of you,' a sentiment faintly echoed by the listening crowd. Security personnel in black uniforms and helmets, some visibly armed, flank Odili, while a man in a blue cap with 'O.U.F.' stands attentively behind him. It is now time for governance,' commending the individual for having 'hit the ground running' and 'touched the critical sectors in less than one year in office.' He concludes by conveying a message from 'our people' to 'stand firm with the president, align with his positive policies.' The background features a banner with 'DELIVERANCE' and 'STRONGHOLDS', dignitaries in traditional robes seated under a tent, and media equipment including a camera on a tripod. segments.integrated_caption: The video captures a live outdoor political event where Peter Odili, the Former Governor of Rivers State, delivers a passionate speech from a bright green podium with a red microphone. He asserts, "the entire map of Nigeria is sitting on Rivers State," and dramatically warns, "When Rivers State coughs, Nigeria will catch cold," which is met with audible cheers and applause from the surrounding crowd. During his address, a man in a vibrant orange traditional shirt with intricate patterns, dark sunglasses, and a beard enters the frame from the left, raising a black ceremonial staff or baton above his head in a gesture of support. Behind Odili, security personnel in black uniforms and helmets stand attentively, while a man in a blue cap with 'O.U.F.' printed on it observes from the right. The background is filled with a diverse crowd of onlookers, and a camera on a tripod indicates media coverage. Emeka Woke, a bald man in a dark gray traditional shirt, speaks passionately into a microphone, gesturing emphatically. His strong, declarative voice criticizes those who focus on Wike's actions rather than governance, asking the audience if Wike is still the governor of Rivers State, to which they respond with a resounding 'No!'. The camera then cuts to Justina Emeji, vibrant in an orange traditional outfit and matching headwrap, who confidently declares her support for 'Chief Barrister Ezenwo Nyesom Wike' and 'Ahmed Bola Tinubu' in a clear, strong voice, thanking the audience for their show of support amidst cheers and clapping. Following her, Prof. Aaron Ojule, a man in a striped shirt and glasses, speaks with a serious, authoritative demeanor and a measured voice from a red chair, discussing a communiqué. He warns the President to 'beware of certain politicians who are currently parading themselves in Rivers State as supporters of the Tinubu administration,' clarifying that 'these politicians, we are supporters of a presidential candidate with whom they aligned.' Throughout, the audience, dressed in traditional and formal attire, listens attentively.

As he speaks, the video cuts to various clinical scenes: a patient being attended to in an emergency room, a surgical team performing an operation, a doctor examining a child with an otoscope, and a consultation between a doctor and a pregnant woman, visually illustrating the comprehensive services. An aerial view of the hospital building in a busy urban area is shown, followed by more clinical interactions, including a doctor examining a patient’s foot, a woman undergoing a mammogram, and a doctor using a stethoscope on a patient. Dr. Shitta-Bey continues, emphasizing, "it really doesn't matter if you're paying out of pocket, using your HMO or private insurance. We focus completely on providing that world-class affordable healthcare for all the family at all times." The segment concludes with a woman receiving a document from a staff member at the reception desk, reinforcing the hospital's commitment to patient care and administrative efficiency, all set against the continuous background music.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment features the Minister of Power, seated indoors in a well-lit, neutral-toned room, dressed in a white traditional shirt and a beige striped cap, wearing glasses, an earpiece, and a lapel microphone. With the 'CHANNELS' logo visible in the top left and the time 22:22 displayed in the bottom right, he speaks directly to the camera, occasionally gesturing, to clarify his previous statements. He explains that his comments were "directed at those that we believe, uh, because of the recent increase in tariff, will start enjoying 20, 22, 24 hours of uh, power supply." He further clarifies that a "tariff for band A, which is for 15% of the total, uh, electricity consumers, will increase by over 200% does not necessarily translate into 200% increase in their electricity bills if power is properly, uh, managed in terms of consumption." Behind him, a decorative plant with reddish-brown foliage is to his left, and a dark wooden door or panel to his right. Throughout his address, various international news headlines scroll across the bottom of the screen under the 'FOREIGN' category, including 'NFL GREAT OJ SIMPSON DIES OF CANCER, AGED 76', 'BIDEN CONSIDERING REQUEST TO DROP ASSANGE CHARGES', 'RUSSIAN STRIKES HIT ODESA ON LIBERATION DAY', 'MALI JUNTA BANS POLITICAL ACTIVITIES AMID TENSIONS', and 'SUDAN ARMY SAYS TO HAND OVER TO SUPPORTERS ONLY'. The Minister then addresses a specific point, stating, "The example of the freezer well, it may might not have gone down well with majority of Nigerians. I will say, oh, sorry about that. It was never in my intention to insult anybody. Uh, it was just out of my passion and uh, my eagerness to ensure that we make a change in this sector, which has suffered a lot of uh, setbacks." This apology reinforces the initial on-screen graphic, briefly seen, 'Minister Of Power Apologises To Nigerians Over Comment'.

The News Anchor's clear, professional voice introduces a report on the electricity tariff hike, noting concerns from Port Harcourt residents and business owners, and introduces correspondent Deborah Agbelamori. Lower third graphics display foreign news headlines such as 'SUDAN ARMY SAYS TO HAND OVER TO SUPPORTERS ONLY' and 'PL TO USE SEMI-AUTOMATED OFFSIDE TECH. NEXT SEASON', with the CHANNELS Logo and time 22:23 consistently visible. A brief cut shows a man in a dark blue traditional outfit and matching cap, speaking against a golden background, as the lower third updates with the sports headline 'BURNLEY MANAGER KOMPANY BANNED FOR TWO GAMES'. The video then shifts to outdoor footage of an electricity substation with metal towers and insulators, followed by close-ups of electrical meters and power lines on buildings. During this, correspondent Deborah Agbelamori's voice reports on Minister Adebayo Adelabu's announcement of a substantial 240% electricity tariff increase, detailing the government's defense that only 1.5 million out of 12 million users fall under the higher tariff category, and her team's efforts to verify if only those with 20 hours of power are feeling the pinch. The lower third simultaneously highlights 'ELECTRICITY TARIFF HIKE' with a sub-headline about non-'Band A' users calling for a reversal, and the NEWS at 10 Logo appears. The segment concludes with street-level views of a Nigerian town, showing vehicles like a yellow tricycle and cars, pedestrians, and buildings with power lines, while sports headlines continue to update, including 'SPORTING'S AMORIM DENIES LIVERPOOL AGREEMENT' and 'MEDVEDEV KNOCKED OUT BY KHACHANOV IN MONTE CARLO'.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with wide shots of a semi-urban Nigerian street scene, identified by a female narrator as 'Lekaya House Estate,' a mix of residential and business premises. The visuals showcase modest buildings, parked cars, tangled overhead power lines, a modern LED streetlight, a pharmacy, and a small shop with a wooden facade and greenery, all while the CHANNELS logo remains in the top left and lower third graphics display headlines like 'MEDVEDEV KNOCKED OUT BY KHACHANOV IN MONTE CARLO'. The narrator explains that residents in this area 'get only three to four hours of power supply daily, but have noticed the high on their prepaid meters.' The camera then transitions to interviews with local residents, each holding a CHANNELS microphone. The first interviewee, a man in a red shirt and a black cap with 'KINGS' embroidered, speaks earnestly, stating that 'the light is not even being given like how it's supposed to be' and that 'the power supply is very, very low.' Next, a bald man in a white patterned shirt, standing in front of a shop with handbags and a wooden sign reading 'REHAB 12123 7DAY', declares that 'This government have failed Nigeria' since 'the arrival of the president, Tinubu,' noting that 'Nothing is going on in this country. Everything is increment, increment, increment.' Following him, a man in a black Nike cap and dark t-shirt gestures expressively while speaking, emphasizing that 'electricity is important' and questioning, 'would we pay 300% increment and get the value of what we are paying?' The segment concludes with a shot of a modern storefront with a glass display and a sign for 'Amala Station', followed by a wider view of the street with a red car parked in front of a yellow building, as the narrator mentions 'Mr. Sam Onos, a business owner around the vicinity,' explaining how 'electricity high bills compounding with scanty power supply, eats...'

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens inside a retail store, showcasing a Hisense refrigerator, a sink, and a window with reflections, setting a commercial scene. Sam Onos, a business owner from Port Harcourt, is then seen in a dark blue t-shirt, speaking into a CHANNELS microphone, identified by a lower third discussing 'ELECTRICITY TARIFF HIKE.' He voices concerns about increased spending on fuel and the lack of electricity affecting business operations and profit. A female narrator's voice then introduces the Nigeria Labour Congress (NLC) making bold statements, with its head of information, Benson Upa, warning of a potential showdown if tariffs aren't reverted. This is followed by a graphic of the Labour Party logo, split red and green with a white gear and three illustrated figures, accompanied by the slogan 'FORWARD EVER.' The scene transitions to Alex Agwunobi, the NLC River State Chairman, who is visually a man in a green polo shirt, gray flat cap, and glasses, seated in a room with patterned curtains and decorative tassels, speaking directly to the camera. He echoes the sentiment, stating that considering the plight of Nigerians struggling with high prices, inflation, and government policies that inflict more poverty and hardship, such actions are unacceptable, and the government should prioritize the well-being of its people. The lower third displays contact hotlines and channel information, promoting Channels TV on DSTV Channel 420, reinforcing the news broadcast's focus on public concern regarding electricity tariffs.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment begins with a female reporter, identified as Debra Abalama of Channels Television News, reporting on how residents and businesses in Port Harcourt are grappling with electricity price hikes and calling for reversals or equitable power distribution. This report is visually accompanied by outdoor street views in a Nigerian town, showcasing tangled overhead power lines, modest buildings, palm trees, and local shops, followed by a close-up of an electricity substation enclosed by a metal fence with barbed wire, and workers in hard hats performing maintenance on a large transformer, illustrating the infrastructure context. The scene then transitions to a news studio where a male News Anchor, dressed in a light gray suit and striped tie, stands before a city skyline backdrop. Lower third graphics display 'ELECTRICITY TARIFF HIKE: Analysing The Impact Of Proposed Increase'. The News Anchor, speaking with a clear, authoritative voice, introduces Channels Television analyst Mr. Laolu Akande, who joins live from Abuja studios to discuss the tariff increase and the intractable problems in the power sector. The broadcast shifts to a split-screen interview format, showing the News Anchor on the left and Mr. Laolu Akande, in a dark blue traditional outfit with glasses and a white beard, on the right. The studio features professional lighting with purple and blue hues and illuminated cityscapes. The lower third continues to display the headline and scrolling news tickers, including 'UMAHI: LAGOS-CALABAR COASTAL ROAD WILL COST N15TR' and 'FG: ATIKU MISLEADING NIGERIANS ON LAGOS-CALABAR ROAD'. Mr. Akande, speaking with a thoughtful, analytical male voice and addressing the anchor as "Hio," begins to analyze the root causes of the power sector's major challenges.

segments.integrated_caption: The video segment opens with a split-screen view, featuring a News Anchor in a light gray suit and striped tie attentively listening on the left, while on the right, Channels TV Analyst Laolu Akande, wearing a dark gray traditional shirt, glasses, and a white beard, speaks with expressive hand gestures. He is seated before a cityscape backdrop bathed in warm, golden-hour lighting. The lower third graphics display the main topic: 'ELECTRICITY TARIFF HIKE: Analysing The Impact Of Proposed Increase'. Laolu's clear, analytical voice immediately delves into the "problematic privatization sale" of the discos and gencos, emphasizing that those who purchased the discos lack both the "technical competence" and the "financial muscle" to invest in the business. The camera transitions to a full-screen view of Laolu, who continues to articulate his points with animated gestures, explaining that this situation has led to a "liquidity crisis" in the sector, characterized by "no cash flow," which discourages investment. He argues that the current attempt to increase the tariff is merely a way for the companies to "solve the liquidity crisis," rather than addressing the fundamental issue of the "faulty privatization sale." Throughout his analysis, scrolling news tickers appear in the lower third, initially showing 'HEADLINES: GUNMEN ATTACK POLICE CHECKPOINT IN EBONYI' and 'BUSINESS: OPEC: NIG. OIL PRODUCTION DROPS TO 1.23 MBPD IN MARCH', which later update to 'DMO TO RAISE NGN450 BILLION IN APRIL BOND AUCTION' and 'DANGOTE: FALLING DIESEL PRICE WILL LOWER NIG'S INFLATION'. The professional studio environment is consistent, featuring the CHANNELS logo in the top left and the 'NEWS at 10' logo in the lower third, maintaining a serious and analytical atmosphere focused on economic and policy implications.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of Laolu Akande, a Channels TV Analyst, a bald man with a white beard, glasses, and a dark gray traditional shirt, seated before a warm, dusk-lit cityscape. He speaks with expressive hand gestures, articulating, "...sometime ago in this country. That is the fundamental problem and it has to be addressed." The lower third displays 'DANGOTE: FALLING DIESEL PRICE WILL LOWER NIG'S INFLATION'. The scene then transitions to a split-screen, showing Laolu on the right and a News Anchor on the left. The News Anchor, in a light gray suit with a blue and white striped tie, is in a studio with a purple-lit cityscape background, asking, "Alright, so there are there are indeed divergent views regarding the electricity tariff hike. But would you say it is justified? We talk about the the the bands for our consumers, band A, band B, and and all and all of that. What would you say are concerning this?" The lower third updates to 'ECB KEEPS INTEREST RATE UNCHANGED AT RECORD 4%'. Laolu Akande responds, animated with hand gestures, explaining, "Yeah, so I think it's important for us Nigerians to understand that now the power sector is a business. And so, uh there has to be a cash flow for the people in the business. I think the government has tried to explain that the recent tariff hike is only for about 15% of the consumers at a particular category. The problem, however, is that the Nigerian people do not see uh the delivery of effective service from..." During his explanation, the lower third changes to 'IMF: GLOBAL FIN. INST. LOST $12BN TO CYBERATTACKS IN 20YRS', then 'FOREIGN NFL GREAT OJ SIMPSON DIES OF CANCER, AGED 76', and finally 'BIDEN CONSIDERING REQUEST TO DROP ASSANGE CHARGES'. The Channels logo remains visible in the top left, and the time '22:28' in the bottom right, maintaining a professional news atmosphere.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Laolu Akande, a bald man with a white beard, glasses, and a dark gray traditional shirt, seated before a cityscape at dusk, speaking animatedly with expressive hand gestures. He discusses the financial challenges of "discos" (distribution companies), noting their lack of "financial muscle" to fix old equipment and their reliance on "estimated billing," which leads to people not receiving adequate services despite paying. The lower third graphics cycle through various international headlines, including 'FOREIGN BIDEN CONSIDERING REQUEST TO DROP ASSANGE CHARGES', 'RUSSIAN STRIKES HIT ODESA ON LIBERATION DAY', 'MALI JUNTA BANS POLITICAL ACTIVITIES AMID TENSIONS', and 'SUDAN ARMY SAYS TO HAND OVER TO SUPPORTERS ONLY', while the CHANNELS logo remains in the top left. At 0:18, Laolu is identified as a 'Channels TV Analyst'. He continues to elaborate on the need for "resources, money coming into that sector," and the government's consideration of increasing tariffs for those who can pay to improve services. Around 0:36, the screen splits, introducing a News Anchor in a light gray suit, white shirt, and blue and white striped tie, seated in a studio with a purple-lit cityscape background, who listens attentively, occasionally nodding. The News Anchor interjects with "Alright, very quickly," prompting Laolu, who then suggests finding "other means of bringing in resources and finances into the sector outside of tariffs." segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a News Anchor, a man in a light gray suit, white shirt, and blue and white striped tie, seated in a studio against a purple-lit cityscape background. He speaks directly to the camera with a serious and composed demeanor, his clear, formal voice announcing the death of Dr. Ogbonnaya Onu, identified as the first civilian governor of old Abia State and former Minister of Science, Technology and Innovation. Initially, lower third graphics display sports headlines like 'PL TO USE SEMI-AUTOMATED OFFSIDE TECH. NEXT SEASON' and 'DJOKOVIC, SINNER INTO MONTE CARLO QUARTER-FINAL', along with the time '22:30'. The CHANNELS Logo is visible in the top left. As the anchor continues, the lower third updates to 'OGBONNAYA ONU (1951-2024) Former Aba Gov. & Federal Minister Dies In Abuja'. The anchor details Onu's background from Ebonyi State, his role as an All Progressives Congress presidential aspirant in the 2023 election, and his passing today in an Abuja hospital. He recounts Onu's appointment as Minister of Science and Technology by former President Muhammadu Buhari in 2015, a position he held until resigning in 2022 to contest the presidential ticket, which he lost to Bola Tinubu. The scene then transitions to a still image of Ogbonnaya Onu, a man in a white traditional outfit and red cap, standing at a wooden podium with microphones, accompanied by the headline 'OGBONNAYA ONU DIES AT 72'. The anchor reports that Governor Francis Nwifuru of Ebonyi State described Onu's death as sad, praising him as a 'thoroughbred politician' and 'advocate of justice and fairness'. The lower third graphics update to highlight CHANNELS as 'Africa's most awarded broadcaster' and a '16-time winner of the BEST TV STATION award'. The segment concludes with another still image of a smiling Ogbonnaya Onu, with the headline 'TINUBU MOURNS OGBONNAYA ONU', as the anchor states President Bola Tinubu has paid tribute and extended condolences to the Onu family. The time updates to '22:31', and a note about CHANNELS' YouTube views exceeding 1.3 billion is displayed, maintaining a somber and respectful tone throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens in a news studio with the Female News Anchor, dressed in a purple blazer and black top, seated at the Studio Set, presenting a report on Plateau IDPs. Behind her, a large screen displays a map of Plateau State with 'Jos' and an image of a Man in Green Cap, while the lower third graphic initially reads 'BUSINESS DMO TO RAISE NGN450 BILLION IN APRIL BOND AUCTION' and the time '22:34'. The scene then transitions to a dry, sunlit rural settlement with dusty ground and modest, damaged buildings, where the distinct sound of destroyed homes is heard. Military personnel in camouflage uniforms and red berets are seen walking alongside civilians and an Official in a dark suit, holding papers, near a convoy of black armored vehicles, including a Toyota with an 'A4' sticker. The CHANNELS Logo remains in the top left corner, and the NEWS at 10 Logo appears briefly. A Male Narrator's voiceover explains that the Plateau State government's task force on IDP resettlement has commenced on-the-spot assessments and interactive sessions with victims across 121 communities in Mangu, Riyom, Bokkos, and Barkin Ladi local government areas. The lower third graphic updates to 'PLATEAU IDPS Govt Plans Resettlement Before Commencement Of Farming Season'. The narrator adds that some displaced persons have started returning with anxiety, urging the government to speedily commence the resettlement agenda. Interviews with local residents follow: Jeff Mato from Maiyanga Community, in a green and white striped polo shirt, earnestly expresses hope that the government will hasten up so people can return for the crucial farming season. Danjuma Mafuyai, a Bokkos Resident in a gray shirt, then pleads for a special consignment of fertilizer for all affected areas, specifically advocating for his community. The Male Narrator concludes by stating that the chairman of the task force led the team to Mangu for their assessment.

segments.integrated_caption: The video segment opens with a group of men, including officials and local residents, gathered in a rural, dusty settlement with mud-brick houses and corrugated metal roofs, assessing damaged structures. A narrator explains they are reviewing preparations for the resettlement of over 67 affected communities. Some individuals hold documents, suggesting a damage assessment process. The scene transitions to an interview with RTD. AVM Christopher Rwang Pam, Chairman of the Resettlement Task Force, who, wearing a gray cap, glasses, and a dark suit, speaks to reporters about the resettlement being done "in phases" and the immediate assignment to "provide security for them to start working on their farms." A man in a red-and-white striped polo shirt and a black cap stands beside him, with a military-style vehicle and sparse vegetation in the background under a bright sky. The footage then cuts to another interview with RTD. Brig. Gen. John Sura, Vice Chairman of the Resettlement Task Force, wearing a black cap, sunglasses, and a dark shirt, speaking under a tree with a clear blue sky behind him. A narrator mentions his team visited Bokkos, where 53 communities were assessed, and Sura details their focus on amenities, stating they are looking at "worship centers," "water points," "clinics," and "schools" to ensure residents' comfort. The video returns to the group inspecting a partially collapsed house, with some individuals in military-style uniforms and others in civilian clothing, including one in a black shirt with 'USAID' and 'PEACE CORPS' logos. The final interview features Monday Kassah, Chairman of Bokkos LGA, wearing a black cap and a brown shirt, speaking in front of a damaged mud-brick wall under an overcast sky. A narrator introduces him, expressing confidence that the displaced persons will soon be resettled, before Kassah adds that they are "engaging the state government to see that we pull resources together" for the displaced. Throughout the segment, the CHANNELS logo is visible in the top left corner, and lower third graphics display foreign news tickers such as 'NFL GREAT OJ SIMPSON DIES OF CANCER, AGED 76' and 'RUSSIAN STRIKES HIT ODESA ON LIBERATION DAY'. The environment is consistently rural and arid, with dry ground, scattered rocks, and sparse trees, indicating a conflict-affected or post-disaster area in Plateau State, Nigeria.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide-angle view of the 'ANNUAL DURBAR' in Bauchi Emirate, identified by on-screen text, a vibrant outdoor cultural event that, according to a male speaker, marks the 110th Durbar since its 1914 inception during the colonial administration. Known as Hawan Bariki, the festival takes place a day after the Eid celebration at the Government House and the New Grand Arena built by the Governor. A large crowd, many in colorful traditional attire, fills the scene as a dynamic procession unfolds to the sounds of traditional music and chanting. Men on horseback, described by the speaker as hunters, warriors, and horse riders, display their gallantry and style in elaborate costumes, carrying ceremonial staffs. The Emir of Bauchi, along with other eminent personalities, including Alhaji Suleiman Muhammad, the Hakimin Kasan Lere, and the Madaki of Bauchi who leads the lineup, are seen observing from a raised platform. The dusty ground, bright sunny sky, rows of white and green tents, and a golden-domed structure in the background complete the ceremonial setting, as the camera captures the energy of the crowd, including men in red robes dancing, and a white pickup truck carrying flag-waving participants, all contributing to a festive atmosphere of cultural pride.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video segment opens with a wide-angle view of a vibrant and bustling outdoor cultural event, identified by the on-screen text as the 'ANNUAL DURBAR' in Bauchi Emirate, where the community pays homage to the Governor. The scene is filled with a large crowd of people, many dressed in colorful traditional attire, including flowing robes, caps, and headwraps in hues of red, green, yellow, and white. A dynamic procession unfolds, featuring men on horseback wearing elaborate costumes and carrying ceremonial staffs, moving through the crowd. Some riders are in bright yellow and red garments, while others wear green and white, creating a visually striking parade. The ground is dusty, and the event is set under a bright, sunny sky, with rows of white and green tents lining the perimeter, suggesting an organized gathering. The camera pans across the scene, capturing the energy and movement of the crowd, including groups of men in red robes dancing and performing traditional rituals. In the background, a golden-domed structure is visible, adding to the ceremonial atmosphere. The footage then cuts to a close-up of dignitaries seated on a raised platform, including men in ornate traditional robes with intricate embroidery and headgear, observing the proceedings. The camera returns to the procession, showing more riders and participants, including a white pickup truck carrying individuals on its flatbed, some waving flags. The overall atmosphere is festive and ceremonial, with a strong sense of cultural pride and community participation.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a vibrant cultural procession, likely the Annual Durbar in Bauchi, as traditional music and crowd sounds fill the air. A female narrator explains that the event showcases "traditional regalia with beautifully adorned horses," and "traditional music and dance performances add to the splendor of the Durbar, aimed at thrilling spectators." Men on horseback, in colorful traditional attire, parade through a dusty arena lined with white and green tents, some carrying ceremonial staffs and flags, accompanied by foot soldiers. Groups of dancers in red robes perform synchronized movements to the rhythmic music, while a white pickup truck carries flag-waving participants. The dense crowd of spectators, some dancing, adds to the festive atmosphere. The narrator continues, noting that "during the event, the governor receives greetings from each of the 55 processions," and "various ethnic groups residing in Bauchi also pay homage, demonstrating the festival's unifying nature." A gunshot-like sound punctuates the scene before the camera shifts to a raised platform where dignitaries in elaborate traditional robes are seated. The Emir of Bauchi, Rilwanu Adamu, dressed in a golden robe, is then shown at a podium. The narrator states he is "speaking in Hausa" and "expresses his deep appreciation to the governor for his leadership." The Emir's voice is heard speaking in Hausa, followed by a male translator stating, "Your Excellency, I wish to appeal to you to continue..." Throughout, Channels TV lower third graphics display information, and the logo remains visible.

Men on horseback, adorned in colorful traditional attire—yellow, red, blue, and white robes with ornate headwear—parade through a dusty arena lined with white and green tents. Some riders carry ceremonial staffs and flags, while others are accompanied by foot soldiers in matching regalia. The camera pans across the crowd, capturing a dense gathering of spectators in traditional clothing, including men in white robes and red caps, some dancing and waving. The procession includes groups of dancers in red robes performing synchronized movements, and a white pickup truck carrying participants waving flags. The scene transitions to a raised platform where dignitaries in elaborate traditional robes, including a man in a golden robe with green accents, are seated. The Emir of Bauchi, Rilwanu Adamu, is then shown at a podium, delivering a speech into a microphone, flanked by officials in traditional attire. The lower third graphics display information about Channels TV, including awards, YouTube views, and hotlines, while the time remains at 22:39. segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of Rilwanu Adamu, the Emir of Bauchi, resplendent in a golden robe with green accents and a matching head covering, delivering a speech at a white podium adorned with microphones. His clear, articulate male voice is heard discussing "developmental strides, especially in the areas of education and healthcare and other things that make life better for the people." He is surrounded by dignitaries in traditional attire. A female narrator then introduces Bala Mohammed, the Governor of Bauchi State (mispronounced as 'Dafna Mohammed'), who is seen in traditional regalia with a gold and white cap, speaking at a green podium bearing the 'Governor Bauchi State' seal. The narrator states he "pledges to continue partnering with the Federal Government for the development of the state as he announces new projects in the offing." The Governor's own clear male voice then elaborates, stating, "Our hospital projects are continuing and we are going to build more. More schools. We have declared emergency on education. We have access a lot of funding from development partners. We are very, very grateful." The camera pulls back to reveal a wide shot of the event, showing the Governor on a tiered platform with a large audience. The scene transitions to a vibrant cultural procession, labeled 'ANNUAL DURBAR', where a large crowd of men in colorful traditional attire dance and parade through a dusty open area, some on horses, others carrying ceremonial staffs and flags, with a white pickup truck moving through the crowd. Tents and a golden-domed structure are visible. The female narrator explains, "The Galadima of Bauchi's procession marks the end of the processions and signals the peak of the event. The Emir's procession." A male voice speaking in a local language is heard over the procession visuals. On-screen text identifies the event as the 'Annual Durbar' and notes the Governor's pledge for collaboration, while lower third graphics display news headlines. The female narrator concludes, "As the Emir leaves the podium and back to the arena on horseback, accompanied by palace..." before the audio cuts off.

segments.integrated_caption: The video segment opens with a close-up of U.S. President Joe Biden, wearing a dark suit, white shirt, blue tie, and dark sunglasses, standing at a podium flanked by American and Japanese flags. segments.integrated_caption: The video features a close-up of Aliko Dangote, Chairman of the Dangote Group, speaking into a microphone at a bright, sunny outdoor event.
